title: NAVER and NVIDIA agree to scale Korean sovereign AI to gigawatt capacity
subtitle: The GAK Sejong data center starts at 55 megawatts in 2027 and ramps toward a 1-gigawatt long-term target, running on NVIDIA's DSX full-stack platform.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: NAVER and NVIDIA announced a joint plan to expand NAVER's sovereign AI infrastructure using NVIDIA's DSX platform, a full-stack toolkit for designing and operating AI factories. The GAK Sejong data center in Sejong, South Korea starts at 55 megawatts in the first half of 2027, scales to 100 megawatts later that year, reaches 200 megawatts in 2028, and targets 1 gigawatt long-term. The build will power NAVER's next-generation HyperCLOVA X models, its Seoul World Model program, and a new agentic AI service platform slated for the second half of 2026.

The DSX pitch from NVIDIA is that a national operator can now buy a reference-designed AI factory rather than assemble one — hardware, networking, orchestration, and reference software as a single package. For NAVER, that means the same operator now hosting Korean-language search and cloud services can also become the domestic host for regulated workloads that cannot leave the country under data-sovereignty rules, targeting government and enterprise customers in Korea and — per NAVER — extending to Europe and the Middle East.

This announcement is best read as the operational half of the $880 billion national plan announced by President Lee this week: the state supplies the industrial policy and the fabs; NAVER supplies the hyperscale data-center capacity; NVIDIA supplies the platform layer. It also signals that 'sovereign AI' — a term that meant something aspirational two years ago — is now a specific product line with capacity commitments and customer segments.

For learners and educators: sovereign AI is the answer to a policy question, not a technical one. The question is 'whose laws govern the model when it makes a decision?' — and the answer countries have converged on is 'whichever country hosts the compute and the weights.' Watch how many national champions ship a hyperscaler-partnered build like this over the next 18 months; it maps the emerging geography of who gets to run what workloads in a fragmenting world.
